metadata.title: Adaptive Therapy Revolution — Managing Cancer Like a Chronic Disease
metadata.color_semantics: teardown: INK #2A1A0E body text; CRIMSON #C8102E key finding/vulnerability; SLATE #545454 structure/neutral
metadata.note: CLI video REQUIRED spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT · B05 CHANGE · B06 OUTPUT · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Adaptive therapy — managing cancer like a chronic disease.

Standard oncology tries to kill every cancer cell. Adaptive therapy deliberately leaves drug-sensitive cells alive — to competitively suppress the resistant ones. The Moffitt abiraterone trial doubled progression-free survival in prostate cancer using this strategy. Why isn't every oncologist doing it?

Research adaptive therapy in cancer: the evolutionary rationale, the Moffitt abiraterone prostate cancer trial, clinical evidence in other cancers, practical barriers to adoption, and settings where it outperforms continuous dosing.

props.command: claude "Research adaptive therapy in cancer: the evolutionary rationale (Gatenby framework), the Moffitt abiraterone prostate cancer trial (Zhang et al. 2017), clinical evidence in other cancers, practical barriers to adoption, and settings where it outperforms continuous dosing. Cite published sources."

beats.narration_text: Python script to analyze trial data and compute a progression-free survival comparison table across cancer types and strategies.

props.code:
TRIALS = [
    {"setting": "Prostate (abiraterone)", "strategy": "adaptive", "PFS_mo": 27.0,
     "control_PFS_mo": 16.8, "status": "Phase 2 complete"},
    {"setting": "Breast (endocrine)", "strategy": "adaptive", "PFS_mo": None,
     "control_PFS_mo": None, "status": "Preclinical only"},
    {"setting": "GBM (TMZ)", "strategy": "adaptive", "PFS_mo": None,
     "control_PFS_mo": None, "status": "Phase 1 recruiting"},
]
for t in TRIALS:
    pfsr = f"{t['PFS_mo']:.1f} vs {t['control_PFS_mo']:.1f} mo" if t['PFS_mo'] else "no RCT data"
    print(f"{t['setting']}: {pfsr} -- [{t['status']}]")

beats.narration_text: Trial output — progression-free survival comparison table: adaptive versus standard dosing across cancer types, with Moffitt trial figures highlighted.
Add a column for each cancer type showing the minimum drug holiday that preserves competitive suppression, with dose-scheduling trial citations.

props.command: claude "Add a column for each cancer type: what is the minimum drug holiday that preserves competitive suppression? Cite any dose-scheduling trials."

beats.narration_text: Revised table with drug holiday column and dose-scheduling evidence ratings for each cancer type.

The competitive suppression logic assumes resistant cells pay a fitness cost — that cost is load-bearing. When the sensitive cells are all dead, the resistant cells fill the niche unopposed. Adaptive therapy buys time by keeping that competition alive.

Read Zhang et al. 2017 in Nature Communications and Strobl et al. 2021 in Nature Cancer. Score each trial's competitive suppression assumption — is the fitness cost of resistance directly measured or inferred from response curves?
Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
